does: This paper proposes sequence-to-sequence reward modeling as an alternative to traditional scalar reward models in RLHF. Instead of training a reward model to output a single scalar score for generated sequences, the method trains the reward model using sequence-to-sequence maximum likelihood estimation, where the model outputs natural language feedback that describes why a response is preferable. The key contribution is replacing the binary scalar MLE objective with sequence MLE, enabling richer, fine-grained evaluative feedback without requiring additional human annotations, models, or training stages. Experiments demonstrate that this approach reduces certain misalignment behaviors and improves overall LLM alignment compared to standard RLHF.
connects: This work builds directly on reinforcement learning from human feedback, which has become the standard approach for aligning large language models following early work on learning from human preferences. The paper addresses a known limitation of RLHF: reward models trained on binary preferences often suffer from distribution shift and biased optimization, causing models to find unexpected generalizations that technically maximize the learned reward but violate intended human values. The approach extends prior work on using language-based feedback and explanation generation, drawing on the insight that natural language can express more nuanced evaluations than scalar scores. It connects to broader research on interpretability and explicit reasoning in reward modeling, building on techniques from conditional text generation and sequence-to-sequence architectures.
enables: This method opens pathways toward more interpretable and controllable reward models that provide explicit reasoning rather than opaque scalar signals. By generating natural language feedback, the approach enables better understanding of why the model prefers certain outputs, supporting both debugging of misaligned behavior and more principled human oversight of the alignment process. Future work could extend this to iterative refinement where language feedback directly informs model retraining, or to multi-stage feedback systems where different aspects of response quality are evaluated sequentially. The framework also suggests broader applications in other domains using preference learning, from recommendation systems to policy optimization, wherever richer feedback than scalar rewards could improve alignment.
